title: The Story of the Stock Market
subtitle: A short narrative tour through how markets came to be
description: A story-driven crash course that follows the invention of the stock market from Amsterdam in 1602 to today's algorithmic trading floors.
targetAudience: Curious readers who want historical context before touching an investment app
objectives: Recognise the key historical turning points that shaped modern markets
Explain why stock exchanges emerged and what problem they originally solved
Spot the human patterns that repeat through every market era

modules.title: Origins in Amsterdam
modules.description: How a single Dutch trading company accidentally invented the modern stock market.
objectives: Trace the birth of tradable shares

lessons.title: The Spice Problem
lessons.description: Why no single merchant could afford a voyage to the Indies — and what they did about it.
lessons.content: In 1602 Amsterdam, a single round-trip to the Spice Islands cost more than most merchants would earn in a lifetime, and nearly half the ships never came back. The Dutch East India Company invented a solution still running today: pool the money across many people, and spread the risk across many voyages.
keyPoints: Individual merchants couldn't absorb the risk of a single voyage
Pooling capital turned catastrophic loss into a statistical problem
The VOC's charter granted a 21-year monopoly — which made shares worth owning

lessons.title: The First Share Certificate
lessons.description: What it looked like to own a tiny slice of an ocean-crossing company.
lessons.content: A VOC share was literally a piece of paper that said: bring this back, and you get a cut of what the ships bring home. Within months, people were trading the papers themselves on the street outside the company office — and the price of those papers started reflecting hopes, fears, and rumours.
keyPoints: The secondary market for shares emerged before the primary issuance was fully sold
Price became a running opinion poll about expected returns
Paper ownership abstracted away the ships, crews, and storms

lessons.title: The Bridge Traders
lessons.description: How a stone bridge in Amsterdam became the world's first stock exchange.
lessons.content: Traders gathered on the Nieuwe Brug at dawn because that's where the mail arrived from the docks, and news from the docks moved the price of shares. The word 'bourse' — still used for European exchanges — comes from this informal bridge market.
keyPoints: Information asymmetry has always been the engine of trading profits
Physical proximity to news source dictated trading advantage for 400 years
The bridge crowd was already inventing futures, shorts, and options by 1610

modules.title: The Tulip and the Lesson
modules.description: The first speculative bubble, and why it matters four centuries later.
objectives: Recognise the anatomy of a financial bubble

lessons.title: Tulipmania
lessons.description: When a single rare tulip bulb cost more than a house on an Amsterdam canal.
lessons.content: At the peak of 1637, a single Semper Augustus bulb traded for ten times a skilled craftsman's annual wage. The bubble didn't pop because flowers stopped being beautiful; it popped because a single no-show at a routine auction broke the spell, and the price structure collapsed within days.
keyPoints: Price detached from any underlying cash flow — a pure narrative asset
The crash was triggered by a local, almost trivial event
The pattern — social contagion, then sudden confidence collapse — repeats in every bubble since

lessons.title: The Mirror
lessons.content: Every generation believes its bubble is different because the asset is different. The tulip traders thought tulips were special; later generations thought the same about railroads, radios, dot-coms, and tokens. The asset changes; the script doesn't.
keyPoints: The narrative is always that 'this time is different'
Leverage and easy credit consistently precede the peak
Survivors are the ones who wrote down their rules before the mania started

quiz.question: What most consistently separates a bubble from a boom?
options: The ratio of price to any underlying cash flow
quiz.explanation: Bubbles detach price from any defensible estimate of future cash flow. The asset and era vary — the disconnect is the constant.
